perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Arizona without regard to conflict of law principles, may not be amended except by a writing signed by both parties, and shall supersede any and all prior discussions and writings between the parties concerning the subject matter. The parties hereby unconditionally consent to the federal courts located in Arizona as the venue in any action arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Arizona, may be amended at any time by EHS or EHN, and shall supersede any and all prior discussions and writings between the parties concerning the subject matter. The parties hereby consent to the courts located in Arizona as the venue in any action arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written amendments signed by both parties and stating that EHS/EHN can amend at any time allows unilateral changes to the contract, potentially harming Dr. Murray. This violates the principle of mutual consent required for contract modifications, which is protected by Arizona contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Law on Contract Modification - Mutual Consent
perturbation.law_citation: E-Z Rent-A-Car v. Marquee Contractors, Inc., 203 Ariz. 301, 305, ¶ 13, 53 P.3d 203, 207 (App. 2002)
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona contract law requires mutual assent for contract modifications. The modification allows EHS/EHN to unilaterally amend the agreement which violates this principle, potentially leaving Dr. Murray vulnerable to detrimental changes without his consent.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 9 - GENERAL LEGAL MATTERS (1) Governing Law, Venue, Amendments and Merger:
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts established contract law principles by allowing unilateral amendments by EHS/EHN. The original text required amendments to be signed by both parties, ensuring mutual consent. The new text removes this requirement, enabling one party to alter the agreement to the potential detriment of the other, which contradicts the principle of mutual consent which is a core requirement for contract modifications. While there are no scraped law snippets, the Perturbation and Law explanations both support the conclusion that the altered text introduces a legally significant contradiction, because it undermines the principle of mutual consent.

perturbation.original_text: Employees, agents and contractors of one party shall not be considered employees, agents or contractors of the other party. Any consent or approval by a party to this Agreement shall be made only by a duly authorized officer of that party.
perturbation.changed_text: Employees of one party shall not be considered employees of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the line 'Any consent or approval by a party to this Agreement shall be made only by a duly authorized officer of that party,' it creates an opportunity where consent could be provided by unauthorized personnel. Arizona law demands that agents act within their authorization.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Agency § 2.03 states an agent (or, in this case, representative of the company) can only bind a principal (the company) if they act within the scope of authority. Without the requirement that only a 'duly authorized officer' can give consent, the company can claim later than the consent given to Dr. Murray was invalid because the person who consented was not duly authorized.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 9 - GENERAL LEGAL MATTERS (4) Consent Required:
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause requiring consent or approval to be made only by a duly authorized officer creates a contradiction under agency law. The modified text now allows potentially unauthorized individuals to provide consent, which directly contradicts the principle that agents must act within their authority to bind the principal. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, "An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party." By removing the 'duly authorized officer' clause, the agreement's validity is jeopardized if an unauthorized person consents, as it could be argued they lacked the necessary authority. This creates a legal loophole where the company can later claim the consent was invalid, undermining the agreement.
